summary: This PR adds support for setting an explicit multipart boundary via headers, which is a useful feature. However, several correctness and security issues were identified: the boundary extraction logic is fragile when boundary values contain `=` characters, quoted boundary values are not unquoted before use, and there is no validation of boundary characters which could enable MIME injection attacks. Test coverage for error branches and edge cases is also incomplete.

findings.title: No test coverage for invalid (non-multipart) content-type header raising ValueError
findings.detail: The branch `if not content_type.startswith('multipart/form-data'): raise ValueError('Invalid content-type header for multipart request')` is entirely uncovered by the test suite. No test passes a non-multipart content-type (e.g. `'application/json'`) together with `files=` to exercise this path. A test such as passing `headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'}` with a multipart request should be added.

findings.title: Boundary extraction using split('boundary=')[-1] is incorrect for values containing '=' and is fragile
findings.detail: The boundary is extracted by splitting on `'boundary='` and taking `[-1]`. This fails for boundary values that contain `=`: e.g., `'boundary=abc=def=ghi'` 	 split('boundary=') 	 gives `['', 'abc=def=ghi']` 	 `[-1]` gives `'ghi'` instead of `'abc=def=ghi'`. The correct approach is `split('boundary=', 1)[1]` (or equivalently `section.strip()[len('boundary='):]`), which is also clearer given that the surrounding code already confirms the prefix exists via `startswith('boundary=')`. Using `[-1]` is both a correctness bug and a fragile, hard-to-read pattern.

findings.title: Boundary value not validated against RFC 2046 character set and not unquoted — enables MIME injection
findings.detail:
Two related issues: (1) RFC 2046 allows quoted-string boundaries, e.g. `boundary="my boundary"`. The current code includes the quote characters verbatim in the MIME boundary marker, producing `--"my boundary"` in the body, which is invalid and will not be parsed correctly by receivers. The fix is to strip surrounding double-quote characters after extraction. (2) More critically, no validation is performed against the RFC 2046 §5.1.1 legal character set for boundary values. A caller who controls the `Content-Type` header can inject a boundary containing CRLF sequences such as `boundary=legit

Content-Disposition: form-data; name=injected`, embedding arbitrary MIME parts into the generated request body. Mitigation: after unquoting, validate that the extracted boundary contains only RFC 2046-legal characters and reject any value outside that set.

findings.title: Header lookup and content-type prefix check are case-sensitive, causing silent failures for non-normalised inputs
findings.detail: Two related case-sensitivity issues: (1) The lookup `'content-type' in headers` / `headers['content-type']` uses a lowercase key. When `encode_multipart_data` is called directly with a plain `dict` such as `{'Content-Type': '...'}`, the lowercase key lookup silently misses the header and boundary extraction is skipped without any error. (2) The subsequent prefix check `content_type.startswith('multipart/form-data')` does not normalise the value to lowercase, so a header value of `'Multipart/Form-Data; boundary=X'` would fail the startswith check and silently fall through without extracting the boundary or raising an error. Both cases should be defended with documented constraints, explicit `.lower()` normalisation, or a case-insensitive wrapper.

findings.title: No test for `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` with a content-type string that has no semicolon
findings.detail: A content-type value of `'multipart/form-data'` (no semicolon, no boundary) takes the fast-fail path where `';' in content_type` is False and `ValueError` is raised immediately. The existing `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` parametrization only uses strings that contain a semicolon, so this specific branch is not directly exercised.

findings.title: No test for precedence when both `boundary` kwarg and a content-type header are supplied
findings.detail: The guard `if headers and boundary is None and 'content-type' in headers` means that when an explicit `boundary` kwarg is passed alongside a `headers` dict containing a `content-type`, the header is silently ignored and the provided `boundary` wins. This precedence rule is untested, leaving the intended behaviour ambiguous and unguarded against future refactoring.

findings.title: New `headers` parameter in `encode_request` and combined `data`+`files` path lack unit-level test coverage
findings.detail: Two related gaps: (1) All existing direct unit-call tests of `encode_request` do not pass a `headers` argument; the new code path is only exercised via higher-level integration tests, making the parameter contract harder to pin. (2) `test_multipart_explicit_boundary` only posts `files` without any `data`; the path with a non-empty `data` dict and an explicit boundary header is untested.

findings.title: Header validation logic placed in the wrong abstraction layer
findings.detail: The `encode_multipart_data` function now performs header inspection and raises `ValueError` for invalid content-types, which is validation/routing logic rather than pure encoding. All other helpers in this file (`encode_urlencoded_data`, `encode_text`, etc.) are pure encoding functions. Consider moving the content-type inspection and validation into `encode_request`, which already routes between encoding strategies, to keep each helper focused on a single concern.

findings.title: Missing boundary in multipart/form-data content-type raises ValueError instead of falling back to auto-generation
findings.detail: When a user supplies a `content-type` header value that starts with `multipart/form-data` but contains no `boundary` parameter, `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` raises a `ValueError` that propagates out of `encode_multipart_data`. This prevents the fallback to an auto-generated boundary that `MultipartStream` would otherwise produce. Whether this is intentional (requiring an explicit boundary when the header is present) or an inadvertent regression of the fallback behaviour should be clarified in documentation or a code comment.

findings.title: Explicit `boundary` parameter not exposed through `Request.__init__`, limiting the public API
findings.detail: `Request.__init__` does not accept a `boundary` parameter and does not forward one to `encode_request`, meaning the only way to use an explicit boundary is via a `content-type` header. This is consistent with pre-PR behaviour (no regression), but the PR's feature goal of allowing an explicit boundary is only achievable via headers, not via a dedicated parameter. This limitation is worth documenting or, if the intent is to expose the full capability, `Request.__init__` should accept and forward a `boundary` argument.

findings.title: Truthy check on `headers` is inconsistent with the file's `is not None` style
findings.detail: `if headers and boundary is None` uses a truthy check on `headers`. An explicitly passed empty `Mapping` is falsy and would be silently ignored. The existing style in this file uses explicit `is not None` checks (e.g., `if content is not None`). Prefer `if headers is not None and boundary is None` for consistency and to avoid surprising behaviour with empty mappings.

findings.title: Missing docstring on new public function `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`
findings.detail: All classes and notable helpers in this module have docstrings (e.g., `DataField`, `FileField`, `MultipartStream`). The new module-level public function `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` has no docstring, which is inconsistent with the surrounding style. A brief docstring describing the function's purpose, parameters, return value, and the `ValueError` it raises would improve maintainability.

findings.title: Inconsistent parametrize and annotation style between existing and new tests
findings.detail: The pre-existing test at line 91 uses a single-element outer tuple without a comma in `@pytest.mark.parametrize`, while the new tests use a plain string form. Additionally, the new tests add `: str` type annotations on test parameters while the surrounding untouched tests do not. Neither is wrong individually, but the inconsistency within the file makes the style harder to follow. New tests should conform to the predominant existing style.

findings.title: Boundary value parsing includes trailing whitespace/characters
findings.detail:
The boundary is extracted by splitting on `'boundary='` and taking the last element (`[-1]`), then stripping leading whitespace but NOT trailing whitespace or other characters. For instance `'multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;'` would yield `'+++ '` (with trailing space) as the boundary bytes. RFC 2046 says boundary parameters must not include trailing whitespace. The test case `"multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;"` is expected to produce boundary `b'+++'`, but this code will produce `b'+++ '` (with trailing space). The section is first split on `;`, so `' boundary=+++ '` → after `.strip()` → `'boundary=+++'` — actually wait: `'multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;'.split(';')` gives `['multipart/form-data', ' boundary=+++ ', '']`. The section `' boundary=+++ '` after `.strip()` becomes `'boundary=+++'`, which starts with `'boundary='`, then `'boundary=+++'.split('boundary=')[-1]` gives `'+++'`. That case is fine.

However, consider `'multipart/form-data; boundary=abc=def'`: splitting on `'boundary='` and taking `[-1]` gives `'def'` instead of `'abc=def'`. Using `[-1]` instead of `[1]` is wrong when the boundary value itself contains `=`. The correct approach is to split on `'boundary='` with `maxsplit=1` and take index `[1]`, not `[-1]`. For example, `'Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=abc=def'` → section is `' boundary=abc=def'` → stripped: `'boundary=abc=def'` → split on `'boundary='`: `['', 'abc=def']` → `[-1]` gives `'abc=def'`, which is correct here. But `'boundary=abc=def=ghi'` → split gives `['', 'abc=def=ghi']`, `[-1]` gives `'ghi'` — wrong! Using `split('boundary=', 1)[1]` would give `'abc=def=ghi'` which is correct. This is a correctness bug for boundary values containing `=`.

findings.title: Header lookup uses bare string key which may fail for case-sensitive dict
findings.detail: The code does `if headers and boundary is None and "content-type" in headers` and then `headers["content-type"]`. The `headers` parameter is typed as `Optional[Mapping[str, str]]`. When called from `encode_request` in `_models.py` line 340, it is passed `self.headers` which is an `httpx.Headers` object (case-insensitive). However, if `encode_multipart_data` is called directly with a plain `dict` as `headers` (e.g., `{"Content-Type": "..."}` with a capital C), the lowercase `"content-type"` lookup will fail to find it and silently skip the boundary extraction. The function signature says `Mapping[str, str]` but does not guarantee case-insensitivity. This is a silent correctness failure for direct callers using mixed-case dict headers.

findings.title: When user-supplied content-type is multipart/form-data without boundary, a ValueError is raised — but boundary=None is then passed on, causing a second error
findings.detail:
Actually, the `raise ValueError` at line 163 prevents the second error. But if `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` raises `ValueError` (line 165, missing boundary), it propagates correctly. However: when the user supplies a `content-type` header value that starts with `multipart/form-data` but has no boundary, the `ValueError` from `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` is raised (correct). But the check at line 161 `if not content_type.startswith("multipart/form-data")` only raises when the content-type is wrong — it does NOT raise when boundary is missing (that is handled by `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`). This logic appears correct.

However: when `boundary` is already explicitly supplied (not None) AND the headers also contain a conflicting `content-type`, the headers-based extraction is skipped (due to `boundary is None` check). This is correct behavior.

Actual issue: when the user's `content-type` is `multipart/form-data` without a boundary AND `boundary` is None, `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` raises ValueError. But then `MultipartStream` would have auto-generated a boundary if we had continued. The error prevents the auto-generation fallback, which may be surprising. This is arguably intended behavior per the PR goal, but it is a potential correctness concern if the intent is to only use the header boundary when present and fall back to auto-generation otherwise.

findings.title: The `boundary` keyword argument is dropped from encode_request call
findings.detail: In `_models.py`, the `Request.__init__` does not accept a `boundary` parameter, so it was never passed to `encode_request` before either. However, the old call was positional: `encode_request(content, data, files, json)` and did not pass `boundary`. The new call is keyword-based and also does not pass `boundary`. This is consistent with the pre-existing behavior (the `boundary` parameter of `encode_request` could only be used by direct callers). This is not a regression introduced by the PR, just worth noting that the explicit `boundary` path through `Request.__init__` is still not exposed.

findings.title: Boundary value taken from last split on 'boundary=' allows injection
findings.detail:
The code splits on `'boundary='` and takes `[-1]`, meaning a crafted value like `boundary=foo=bar` yields `bar` instead of `foo=bar`. More critically, because the raw value is taken verbatim from the header without stripping quotes or verifying it contains only RFC-2046-legal characters (alphanumerics and `'()+_,-./:=? '`), an attacker who controls the `content-type` header (e.g. a server-side request that forwards a user-supplied header) can inject an arbitrary byte sequence as the boundary. This boundary is then embedded literally in the generated multipart body (`--<boundary>
` per-part). A boundary containing CRLF sequences such as `boundary=legit

Content-Disposition: form-data; name=injected` would allow a caller to inject extra MIME parts into the request. Mitigation: validate the extracted boundary against the character set defined in RFC 2046 § 5.1.1 (stripping optional surrounding quotes first) and reject any value outside that set.

findings.title: Case-sensitive content-type prefix check may silently skip extraction
findings.detail: The check `if not content_type.startswith('multipart/form-data')` is compared against a lowercased key lookup (`headers['content-type']`), so for headers like `headers` object that normalises to lowercase the prefix check is fine. However, HTTP header *values* are not necessarily lowercase; a header value of `Multipart/Form-Data; boundary=X` would pass the `startswith` check (False → no error) but the value begins with an uppercase character that does not match, silently falling through without boundary extraction and handing `None` to `MultipartStream`. This edge case should be guarded with `.lower()` on `content_type` before the prefix check.

findings.title: Boundary value not stripped of surrounding quotes before encoding
findings.detail: RFC 2046 allows the boundary parameter to be a quoted-string, e.g. `boundary="my boundary"`. The current implementation encodes the raw value including the quote characters into the actual MIME boundary marker, producing `--"my boundary"` in the body, which will not be parsed correctly by receivers. While primarily a correctness issue, the mismatch between the declared boundary (also carrying the quotes) and the actual delimiter could in edge cases lead to unexpected parsing behaviour by multipart consumers. The fix is to strip surrounding double-quote characters from the extracted value.

findings.title: Overly complex split to extract boundary value
findings.detail: Using `.split("boundary=")[-1]` works but is fragile and harder to read than a simple `.replace("boundary=", "", 1).strip()` or a partition call. The surrounding code (e.g., `section.strip().startswith("boundary=")`) already confirms the prefix exists, so a straightforward `section.strip()[len("boundary="):]` would be clearer and more consistent with that check.

findings.title: Missing docstring on new public function
findings.detail: All class methods and classes in this file have docstrings (e.g., `DataField`, `FileField`, `MultipartStream`). The new `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` function is a module-level public function but has no docstring, which is inconsistent with the surrounding style.

findings.title: Validation logic for content-type placed in wrong layer
findings.detail: The `encode_multipart_data` function now does header inspection and validation that feels more like request-building logic. Existing helpers in this file (`encode_urlencoded_data`, `encode_text`, etc.) deal purely with encoding concerns, not header validation. The content-type check and `ValueError` raise sit awkwardly alongside pure encoding code, making the function harder to understand at a glance. Consider pulling the header inspection into `encode_request` (which already routes between encoding strategies) to keep each helper focused.

findings.title: Truthy check on a Mapping could silently skip an empty headers dict
findings.detail: `if headers and boundary is None` uses a truthy check on `headers`. An empty `Mapping` is falsy, so an explicitly passed empty dict would be silently ignored. The existing style in this file uses explicit `is not None` checks (e.g., `if content is not None`). Prefer `if headers is not None and boundary is None` for consistency.

findings.title: Inconsistent parametrize tuple style with surrounding tests
findings.detail: The pre-existing test at line 91 uses `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("key"), ...)` (single-element outer tuple without a comma, effectively just a string), while the new tests at lines 45 and 75 use `@pytest.mark.parametrize("header", [...])` (a plain string). Neither is wrong, but the pre-existing form is inconsistent with itself and the new additions should be consistent with *each other*, which they are. However, note that line 91 also lacks a type annotation on its parameter while the new tests add `: str` annotations — a minor inconsistency with the existing untouched tests (lines 20, 108, etc.) which also lack annotations.

findings.title: Invalid content-type header (non-multipart) raises ValueError — no test coverage
findings.detail: The branch at line 161 (`if not content_type.startswith('multipart/form-data')`) raises a ValueError with the message 'Invalid content-type header for multipart request'. No test in `tests/test_multipart.py` (or any other test file) exercises this path by passing a non-multipart content-type header (e.g. `application/json`) together with `files=`. The `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` test only covers a missing boundary, not a wrong content-type prefix.

findings.title: No test for `encode_multipart_data` when `boundary` is already set and `headers` also contains a content-type
findings.detail: The guard condition `if headers and boundary is None and 'content-type' in headers` means that when an explicit `boundary` kwarg is passed alongside a headers dict containing a content-type, the header is silently ignored and the provided `boundary` wins. This interaction is not tested anywhere, leaving ambiguity about the intended precedence.

findings.title: `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` — no test for content-type without semicolon at all
findings.detail: The function's fast-path for strings without any `;` (the outer `if ';' in content_type` is False) goes directly to `raise ValueError(...)`. A content-type string like `'multipart/form-data'` (no semicolon, no boundary) hits this path. The existing `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` parametrization only uses strings that contain a semicolon, so this branch is not directly unit-tested against `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` itself.

findings.title: `encode_request` — `headers` parameter not exercised via direct unit-call
findings.detail: All existing direct `encode_request(...)` unit-call tests (e.g. `test_multipart_encode`, `test_multipart_encode_non_seekable_filelike`) do not pass a `headers` argument. The new parameter is only tested indirectly through the higher-level `httpx.Client` integration tests. A unit-level test passing `headers` directly to `encode_request` would better pin the contract of the new parameter.

findings.title: No test for explicit boundary when `data` is also provided alongside `files`
findings.detail: `test_multipart_explicit_boundary` only posts `files` without any `data`. The `encode_multipart_data(data or {}, files, boundary, headers)` path with a non-empty `data` dict and an explicit boundary header is untested. A test combining both `data` and `files` with a custom content-type boundary would improve coverage of the combined path.

findings.title: No test coverage for invalid (non-multipart) content-type header raising ValueError
findings.detail: The branch `if not content_type.startswith('multipart/form-data'): raise ValueError('Invalid content-type header for multipart request')` is entirely uncovered by the test suite. No test passes a non-multipart content-type (e.g. `'application/json'`) together with `files=` to exercise this path. A test such as passing `headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'}` with a multipart request should be added.

findings.title: Boundary extraction using split('boundary=')[-1] is incorrect for values containing '=' and is fragile
findings.detail: The boundary is extracted by splitting on `'boundary='` and taking `[-1]`. This fails for boundary values that contain `=`: e.g., `'boundary=abc=def=ghi'` 	 split('boundary=') 	 gives `['', 'abc=def=ghi']` 	 `[-1]` gives `'ghi'` instead of `'abc=def=ghi'`. Using `[-1]` is both a correctness bug and a fragile, hard-to-read pattern.

findings.detail:
Two related issues: (1) RFC 2046 allows quoted-string boundaries, e.g. `boundary="my boundary"`. The existing `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` parametrization only uses strings that contain a semicolon, so this specific branch is not directly exercised.

Additionally, the new tests add `: str` type annotations on test parameters while the surrounding untouched tests do not. Neither is wrong individually, but the inconsistency within the file makes the style harder to follow. New tests should conform to the predominant existing style.
